title: Japanese Curry Rice from Scratch

ingredients.name: potato, peeled and cut into 3 cm chunks
butter
visual_checks: split as 2 tbsp for the vegetables and 2 tbsp for the roux
ingredients.notes: split as 2 tbsp for the vegetables and 2 tbsp for the roux

subs.name: 3 tbsp oil for a dairy-free version — the roux will be slightly less silky but very acceptable

ingredients.name: maida (plain flour)
visual_checks: forms the roux that thickens the curry
ingredients.notes: forms the roux that thickens the curry

ingredients.name: curry powder (mild)
visual_checks: use a mild blend; Japanese S&B brand if available, or any mild curry powder
ingredients.notes: use a mild blend; Japanese S&B brand if available, or any mild curry powder

subs.name: equal parts cumin powder, coriander powder and turmeric with a pinch of cardamom — gives a warming spice profile that reads as Japanese-ish
subs.notes: avoid hot chilli powder; Japanese curry is intentionally mild

subs.name: water plus a stock cube or 1 tbsp soy sauce for a simpler but still flavourful base

ingredients.name: soy sauce
visual_checks: adds umami depth characteristic of Japanese curry
ingredients.notes: adds umami depth characteristic of Japanese curry

ingredients.name: honey
visual_checks: authentic Japanese touch; gives the curry a round, gentle sweetness
ingredients.notes: authentic Japanese touch; gives the curry a round, gentle sweetness

subs.name: 1 tsp sugar or a small piece of grated apple (if available) for the characteristic sweetness

steps.text: Melt 2 tbsp butter in a heavy-bottomed pot over medium heat. Add sliced onions and cook for 12 to 15 minutes, stirring every few minutes, until deep golden and beginning to caramelise. This long cook unlocks the sweetness that defines Japanese curry.
Add potato and carrot pieces and stir for 2 to 3 minutes, coating them in the buttery onion base. Pour in about 100 ml of the stock and scrape up any caramelised bits stuck to the pot bottom.

In a separate small saucepan, melt the remaining 2 tbsp butter over medium-low heat. Add flour and stir constantly for 2 minutes until it turns a pale biscuit colour and smells slightly nutty. Add curry powder and cook for 30 more seconds until fragrant — this is your scratch roux.
Pour the remaining stock gradually into the roux, whisking or stirring vigorously after each addition to prevent lumps. Once smooth, pour this curry sauce into the vegetable pot. Stir to combine.

Bring to a gentle simmer, add soy sauce and honey, cover loosely and cook for 20 to 25 minutes until the potato and carrot are completely tender when pierced with a knife. Stir every 5 minutes and adjust heat so the curry barely bubbles — it burns if left unattended.

Taste and adjust salt. The curry should be thick enough to coat the back of a spoon; if too thin, simmer uncovered for 5 more minutes. Serve in a generous pool next to a mound of steamed rice on the same plate — Japanese curry is always served beside the rice, not on top.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: butter → 3 tbsp oil for a dairy-free version — the roux will be slightly less silky but very acceptable (removes dairy allergen); curry-powder → equal parts cumin powder, coriander powder and turmeric with a pinch of cardamom — gives a warming spice profile that reads as Japanese-ish (avoid hot chilli powder; Japanese curry is intentionally mild); honey → 1 tsp sugar or a small piece of grated apple (if available) for the characteristic sweetness; stock → water plus a stock cube or 1 tbsp soy sauce for a simpler but still flavourful base
indian_kitchen_adaptation: A heavy-bottomed kadai works well for the caramelised onion base. The roux step is identical to making bechamel (white sauce) — any Indian cook who has made pasta bake knows this technique. Japanese mild curry powder (S&B brand) is available at specialty stores in India; or build a DIY mild blend with equal parts jeera powder, dhania powder, haldi and a pinch of elaichi. Amul butter works perfectly.
